Catalog Description

Simulation Methods and Applications. (2-3). Credit 3. Fundamental methodologies

of simulation modeling; random number and variate generation, statistical analysis

of model output, and discrete event modeling using a commercial simulation

language. Prerequisite: STAT 212 or STAT 601.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  STAT 212 or STAT 601.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Understand the fundamental methodologies of discrete event (process-

oriented) simulation modeling.

Understand the key statistical issues involved in simulation data preparation

and the analysis of simulation output.

Become familiar with modeling using both a general-purpose language

(Python) and a commercial language (Simio).

Special Course Designation

None

Grading Policy

Grades will be based on attendance, participation, lab assignments, quizzes, a

midterm exam, and a final exam:

Attendance 4%

Participation 6%

Homework: 0%

Lab Assignments: 10%

Quizzes: 10%

Midterm Exam: 35%

Final Exam: 35%

Attendance:  Attendance will be taken during each lecture and is worth 4% of the

final grade. You will receive 4% if you attend at least four-fifths of the lectures, 3% if

you attend between three-fifths and four-fifths of the lectures, 2% if you attend

between two-fifths and three-fifths of the lectures, 1% if you attend between one-

fifth and two-fifths of the lectures, and 0% if you attend fewer than one-fifth of the

lectures. Excluding exam dates, there are expected to be about 28-29 lectures in

the term.

Participation:  Your participation in these lectures will be worth 6% of the final

grade. You will receive 1% every time you ask/answer a question, up to 6 times

throughout the semester. In any given class session, you can earn up to a maximum

of 1%, i.e., asking/answering more than one question in a lecture will only count as

one point of participation.

Homework:  There will be (roughly) bi-weekly homework assignments. Assignments

and solutions will be posted as Jupyter notebooks on Canvas. Homework will not be

collected or graded.

Lab Assignments:  There will be exercises to be completed during the weekly labs.

These will be graded for completion, not correctness. You will receive 1 point for

each completed lab exercise up to a maximum of 10 points, i.e., you are expected

to complete 10 of the lab exercises.

Quizzes:  There will also be short quizzes given during the weekly labs. These will be

graded for correctness. Your 10 highest quiz scores will be counted.

Midterm and Final Exam:  The midterm and final exams will consist of free-

response questions testing critical thinking and quantitative reasoning. The

to complete 10 of the lab exercises.

Quizzes:  There will also be short quizzes given during the weekly labs. These will be

graded for correctness. Your 10 highest quiz scores will be counted.

Midterm and Final Exam:  The midterm and final exams will consist of free-

response questions testing critical thinking and quantitative reasoning. The

midterm exam is scheduled for  Monday, March 16  from  1:50-3:50pm  in ETB 2026.

The final exam is scheduled for  Thursday, April 30  from  12:30pm-2:30pm  in ZACH

345. If you are unable to take the exams at the scheduled times for a valid reason

(e.g., serious illness or family emergency), you must inform me as soon as possible

and provide proof.

Grades:  Grades will be maintained in the gradebook within Canvas. Any

disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material must be

discussed within five business days of the return of the graded material. No grade

review request will be accepted beyond this period.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Additional Course Information

Copyright:  All documents used in this course are copyrighted. Here, “documents”

means all materials generated for this class, including, but not limited to, syllabi,

slides, homework and lab assignments, solutions, and all materials appearing on

the class website. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the

right to copy any of them for any purpose other than your own personal academic

use unless I expressly grant permission. In particular, course materials may not be

given or sold to any for-profit enterprise. Any use of or upload to the website

Coursehero (or similar) for course materials will be treated as an academic code

violation.
